old_claim.note: Recorded Music Industry provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because the 1905 cinema distribution scope is about silent-era motion-picture prints, production, distribution, and exhibition networks, not commercial recorded-music distribution.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica describes early motion-picture production and distribution by film firms such as Pathé and Gaumont without making recorded-music commerce a prerequisite.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds the target node in motion-picture production/distribution, not in commercial recorded-music catalogs or playback.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Music accompaniment could be exhibition context, but the recorded-music industry is a separate phonograph/copyright market and is not evidenced as required for silent-era film-print distribution.

ontology_before: Industrial Cinema Distribution depended directly on Recorded Music Industry, implying phonograph-record commerce enabled early film distribution.
ontology_after: Industrial Cinema Distribution is modeled through motion-picture film production/projection; recorded music remains a separate entertainment-distribution industry.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_cinema_distribution no longer directly depends on recorded_music_industry.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to synchronized-sound cinema distribution after recorded sound systems became standard.
A stronger source shows early 1905 film distribution depended on commercial recorded-music catalogs or phonograph playback infrastructure.
A new synchronized_sound_cinema_distribution node is added where recorded sound or recorded music becomes a direct dependency.
short_reason: Recorded music is not a sourced prerequisite for silent-era film distribution.
